text.title: Tokio Concurrency Patterns
text.summary: Semaphore-bounded batch processing, channel selection, graceful shutdown patterns.

rule.statement: Use a `Semaphore` to bound concurrent tasks in batch processors. Unbounded `spawn` floods the runtime and DB connection pool.
rule.rationale: Spawning 10,000 tasks simultaneously exhausts connection pool limits and system resources. A semaphore enforces a concurrency ceiling while still processing all items.

rule.statement: Choose the correct channel type for the communication pattern: `mpsc` for work queues, `broadcast` for fan-out events, `watch` for "latest value" (config reload), `oneshot` for request/response.
rule.rationale: Using `broadcast` for a work queue drops messages when the buffer is full instead of backpressuring. Using `mpsc` for fan-out requires cloning receivers, which is awkward. Each channel type is optimized for its pattern.

rule.statement: Use `parking_lot::Mutex` for short critical sections. Use `tokio::sync::Mutex` only when you must hold the lock across an `.await`. Never hold `std::sync::Mutex` across `.await`.
rule.rationale: `std::sync::Mutex` is not `Send`-safe across yield points. Holding it across `.await` in a multi-thread runtime deadlocks the task. `parking_lot` is non-poisoning and faster for sync sections.
